reason: The text never specifies the importance type used by the ranger random forest (it just says 'average feature importance' over 5 CV folds); ranger requires an explicit importance argument (impurity, impurity_corrected, or permutation) and the paper names none, so impurity-importance use cannot be confirmed from the text alone. Selection of the top-11 variables per omics block is corroborated by held-out performance of downstream models trained on exactly that selected set: e.g. the bacteria+MetaCyc feature set achieves AUC 0.87 via cross-validated test folds, evidence that this specific chosen feature set (not just an unrelated model) carries real predictive signal. Finally, the only biological interpretation tied to the selection ('6 pathways were related to purine nucleoside biosynthesis... critical for growth of bacteria in human blood') is a brief, explicitly hedged discussion aside ('the causal relationship... requires further investigation'), not a central, ranking dependent conclusion; the paper's central claims are about comparative AUC performance across four ML algorithms and omics blocks, which do not depend on the correctness of the RF importance ranking or magnitude.
